segments.title: Soviet missile cruisers served distinct fleet roles
segments.question: Were the Slava, Kara, Kresta, and Kresta II classes intended as guided-missile fleet cruisers?
segments.answerShort: Broadly yes, although Dr. Clarke points viewers to his Soviet Navy series because the classes and their fleet roles need more detail than a short answer permits.
segments.summary: A concise orientation places several Soviet missile-cruiser projects within the fleet-cruiser family while warning that their individual roles differ.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke accepts the broad classification but avoids treating the projects as interchangeable. The useful takeaway is that Soviet cruiser labels conceal different anti-air, anti-submarine, and surface-strike emphases that require class-by-class study.

evidence.note: The question names four Soviet cruiser projects; the answer gives a qualified yes and directs viewers to the detailed Soviet Navy series.

segments.title: An early Japanese fleet collapse could redirect Allied priorities
segments.question: Would reducing the Japanese fleet to a few destroyers by January 1942 help or hurt long-term American war support?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke argues it could complicate strategy rather than end the war, potentially encouraging an Asia-Pacific-first sequence even though Germany had already declared war on the United States.
segments.summary: This alternate-history answer connects an early naval victory in the Pacific to the political ordering of the wider Allied war.
segments.body: Destroying Japan's main fleet would not remove the work of retaking occupied territory and restoring communications. Dr. Clarke suggests it could change the order of effort, but Hitler's declaration of war would still keep the European conflict on the American agenda.

evidence.note: The answer weighs an Asia-Pacific-first sequence against Germany's declaration of war on the United States.

segments.title: Ulyanovsk would complement rather than immediately replace the Kievs
segments.question: Were two Ulyanovsk-class carriers intended to replace the Kiev-class aviation ships?
segments.answerShort: No; Soviet practice favored retaining older ships, and Dr. Clarke instead imagines the classes operating in complementary task groups until a later successor replaced the Kievs.
segments.summary: A short force-structure answer explains why new Soviet carriers would not automatically retire the Kiev class.
segments.body: The answer emphasizes institutional practice as much as ship capability: the Soviet Navy was reluctant to discard usable hulls. Ulyanovsk and Kiev therefore make more sense as parallel task-group assets, with replacement deferred to a later carrier generation.

evidence.note: The answer says the Soviet Navy would retain the Kievs and pair the classes until a later successor appeared.

segments.title: Scaling Scharnhorst for triple 15-inch turrets
segments.question: How large might a Scharnhorst-derived battleship become with three triple 15-inch turrets and a thicker belt?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke estimates roughly 244 meters overall and about 31 meters of beam, arguing that extra length matters more than a dramatic beam increase because the original hull was already broad.
segments.summary: A worked design estimate shows how turret geometry, hull form, magazines, and armor affect the size of a larger Scharnhorst derivative.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats the ship as an elliptical, already-beamy design and estimates about nine meters of added length with roughly one meter of added beam. He stresses that the turret and magazine arrangement drives the dimensional change more directly than the extra belt armor.

evidence.note: The answer works through hull form, turret placement, beam, and length to reach an approximate 244-by-31-meter design.

segments.title: How early the cavity magnetron might be developed
segments.question: How early could the cavity magnetron plausibly have been invented?
segments.answerShort: The 1920s are the earliest plausible window, but only if German research circulated sooner and received sustained funding instead of being neglected until the mid-1930s.
segments.summary: A technology-history answer separates theoretical possibility from the publication, materials, and funding chain needed for a usable cavity magnetron.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke places the earliest plausible development in the 1920s, not because the physics was unknowable but because the research path was fragmented. Earlier circulation of German work and a stronger institutional push would be necessary to turn proposals into a practical device.

evidence.note: The answer identifies a 1920s theoretical window and explains the publication and funding changes required.

segments.title: Talwar and Gorshkov frigates follow different production paths
segments.question: Would Talwar- and Gorshkov-derived ships have been Russia's last frigates without newer technology?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke distinguishes the Indian-influenced Talwar program from Russia's domestic Gorshkov line and its enlarged derivatives, making a simple last-class conclusion misleading.
segments.summary: This answer untangles Russian and Indian frigate lineages, shipyards, long build cycles, and the emergence of enlarged Gorshkov variants.
segments.body: The Talwar family reflects a blended Russian-Indian design and production history, while the Gorshkov class is a Russian domestic program that continues into modified and enlarged forms. Dr. Clarke also warns that long Russian construction times can make class chronology look simpler than the actual industrial overlap.

evidence.note: The answer contrasts Talwar's Indian connection with Gorshkov production and enlarged follow-on designs.

segments.title: Converting the Deutschland ships into light carriers
segments.question: What air group could the three Deutschland-class ships carry if converted into carriers before the war?
segments.answerShort: A conversion comparable to a light-fleet carrier might carry about eighteen fighters and eighteen strike or reconnaissance aircraft, though the exact result depends on how extensive the rebuild is.
segments.summary: A compact carrier-conversion estimate links hull size and conversion depth to a plausible German light-carrier air group.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames the answer around the conversion standard rather than treating the hull as a fixed aircraft count. A substantial light-carrier rebuild could support roughly three dozen aircraft, split between fighters and strike or reconnaissance machines.

evidence.note: The answer estimates a light-carrier-style group of roughly eighteen fighters plus eighteen other aircraft.

segments.title: Why flank speed means more than full speed
segments.question: What does the naval order flank speed mean, and do other navies use an equivalent?
segments.answerShort: It is mainly American terminology for pushing machinery beyond ordinary full speed, while Royal Navy practice would more likely call for maximum revolutions and all valves open.
segments.summary: A terminology answer traces flank speed to high-speed maneuver and compares American wording with British engineering orders.
segments.body: Flank speed denotes an emergency or maximum effort beyond routine full speed, historically associated with fast forces moving around an enemy's flank. Other navies may use different words, but their engineers understand equivalent orders such as maximum revolutions with all valves open.

evidence.note: The answer defines American flank-speed usage and gives a Royal Navy machinery-order equivalent.

segments.title: Triple and quadruple turrets were feasible before 1914
segments.question: When should triple and quadruple naval gun turrets become available in an alternate design timeline?
segments.answerShort: Triple turrets were understood by about 1908, while French quadruple-turret work was underway by 1913-1914, placing both concepts before the First World War.
segments.summary: A short design-history answer gives practical dates for introducing multi-gun capital-ship turrets.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats triple turrets as established technology by roughly 1908 and quadruple arrangements as active French design work just before the First World War. The limiting question is therefore adoption and engineering development, not whether the concepts existed.

evidence.note: The answer dates triple-turret understanding to about 1908 and French quadruple work to 1913-1914.

segments.title: County-class cruiser design depends on treaty limits
segments.question: With 14,000 tons of standard displacement, what speed, armament, and armor would the Royal Navy design for a County-class cruiser under the Washington Treaty?
segments.answerShort: The gun and displacement limits define the design: an eight-inch cap suggests twelve guns in four triples, while looser limits could support 9.2-inch guns and more armor.
segments.summary: A hypothetical 14,000-ton County-class cruiser shows how treaty gun and displacement limits drive Royal Navy design choices.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats speed, armor, and armament as treaty-constrained variables rather than independent preferences. With an eight-inch limit he expects four triple turrets and as much armor as the speed requirement permits; a larger gun allowance opens a 9.2-inch alternative.

evidence.note: The answer derives armament and armor choices from treaty gun, speed, and displacement limits.

segments.title: Bulges, machinery, and speed in a dreadnought rebuild
segments.question: What speed could a 26-knot prewar dreadnought retain after 1930s re-engining and torpedo-defense improvements?
segments.answerShort: The result could range from about 24 to 28 knots depending on power, external versus internal bulges, and whether saved machinery volume is used for protection rather than speed.
segments.summary: A rebuild answer shows why modern machinery alone cannot predict a battleship's post-refit speed.
segments.body: External bulges increase protection and stability but worsen the hull's length-to-beam relationship, while internal protection may use machinery space without changing the outer hull. Dr. Clarke therefore makes speed conditional on both the new power plant and the chosen torpedo-defense geometry.

evidence.note: The answer compares internal and external bulging and gives a conditional 24-28-knot range.

segments.title: Earlier cavity magnetrons reshape radar warfare
segments.question: How would an earlier and more widespread cavity magnetron change the Second World War?
segments.answerShort: Earlier compact radar would most benefit states able to mass-produce and display it effectively, especially Britain and later the United States, with major consequences for air warning, ships, aircraft, and submarine detection.
segments.summary: This answer connects magnetron technology to radar displays, industrial scale, Chain Home, maritime aircraft, and anti-submarine warfare.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that the decisive advantage is not the component alone but the ability to manufacture systems and present contacts usefully to operators. Earlier compact radar could improve ship and aircraft search, expose submarines and periscopes, and amplify Britain's prewar radar infrastructure before American mass production expands the advantage.

evidence.note: The answer links compact radar to display technology, British infrastructure, American production, and submarine detection.

segments.title: Why an Anglo-German naval treaty was politically implausible
segments.question: Could Britain and Germany have negotiated a naval treaty before the First World War?
segments.answerShort: Not realistically: Britain distrusted Germany's naval challenge, Germany demanded recognition of its place in the sun, and neither side had the political maturity or shared assumptions needed for a durable settlement.
segments.summary: A diplomatic answer explains why fleet ratios alone could not overcome Anglo-German mistrust and incompatible ambitions.
segments.body: Britain saw a formerly close power turning into a direct strategic threat, while Germany wanted status and concessions that would acknowledge it as a leading world power. Dr. Clarke also notes that even Britain's arrangements with France and Russia were understandings rather than a simple alliance template that could be copied.

evidence.note: The answer stresses mistrust, German world-power ambitions, and incompatible political assumptions.

segments.title: Why early cruisers carried guns wherever they could
segments.question: Why did turn-of-the-century cruisers cram guns around the hull before centerline mounts became standard?
segments.answerShort: With fragile protection, manual fire control, and poor stabilization, designers valued the largest possible volume of fire to disable an opponent before their own ship was hit.
segments.summary: A design-doctrine answer connects scattered cruiser batteries to weak protection and pre-director fire control.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke calls these cruisers glass-jawed ships: a serious hit could quickly put them in trouble. Without modern stabilization or centralized fire control, many guns increased the chance of landing disabling hits first, even when the layout was inefficient by later standards.

evidence.note: The answer links numerous gun positions to manual fire control, weak protection, and a need to hit first.

segments.title: Removing Hipper's rear turrets does not yield free speed
segments.question: How much speed would an Admiral Hipper-class cruiser gain by removing both rear main turrets?
segments.answerShort: Probably less than expected, because the ship would need ballast or a wider rearrangement to restore trim; displacement and speed do not relate as simply as vehicle weight on land.
segments.summary: A cruiser-modification answer explains why deleting heavy turrets can create balance problems instead of a straightforward speed gain.
segments.body: The rear turrets are part of a carefully balanced hull, so removing them shifts weight distribution and may require ballast that gives back much of the apparent saving. Dr. Clarke stresses that ship speed depends on hull form, displacement, and power rather than weight reduction alone.

evidence.note: The answer explains trim and ballast consequences and rejects a simple weight-to-speed calculation.

segments.title: A unified European navy would need global carrier coverage
segments.question: How many carrier groups would make sense for a unified European Union navy?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke estimates roughly six to eight groups to sustain presence in the Mediterranean, North Atlantic, and Indo-Pacific while allowing maintenance and rotation.
segments.summary: A force-structure sketch derives a European carrier requirement from sea borders, overseas territories, deployment cycles, and three persistent theaters.
segments.body: Existing French, Italian, and Spanish aviation ships provide a starting point, but the strategic requirement comes from geography rather than simply adding current fleets. Sustaining three deployed groups would need at least six hulls and preferably eight so long-distance Indo-Pacific coverage and deep maintenance do not collapse the schedule.

evidence.note: The answer identifies three standing regions and derives a six-to-eight-carrier rotation.

segments.title: Old ships' boats may survive without a clear pedigree
segments.question: Could small boats on British waterways unknowingly descend from Royal Navy ships' boats from the age of sail?
segments.answerShort: Yes, but proving it would be extremely difficult because thousands were built, repeatedly repaired, and followed a long-lived standard pattern from roughly 1760 to 1960.
segments.summary: A maritime-survival answer explains why an old rowing boat may have naval ancestry that is almost impossible to document.
segments.body: Even small warships carried several boats, large ships carried many more, and dockyards produced additional craft for local work. The Royal Navy's durable boat patterns changed only incrementally, so physical resemblance can span two centuries without supplying a traceable identity.

evidence.note: The answer cites the enormous production volume and continuity of Royal Navy small-boat patterns.

segments.title: Earlier refrigeration improves endurance more than tactics
segments.question: How would practical refrigeration without modern technology change earlier maritime operations?
segments.answerShort: It would improve food quality, reduce reliance on salting and faulty canning, and help long voyages and winter survival, but it would not by itself transform naval tactics.
segments.summary: A logistics answer links earlier refrigeration to healthier crews, safer provisioning, and longer-distance food movement.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke expects happier crews and fewer food-preservation hazards, including some risks associated with early canned supplies. The wider effect would be better winter survival and longer food distribution networks, with population and expeditionary consequences rather than a single decisive naval change.

evidence.note: The answer discusses salting, canning, expedition survival, and long-distance food transport.

segments.title: A G3 carrier conversion would resemble a British Lexington
segments.question: What kind of carrier would a G3 battlecruiser conversion produce, and how would it compare with an Admiral-class conversion?
segments.answerShort: It would be a roughly 35,000-ton fleet carrier comparable to Lexington, probably with a single large hangar, substantial dual-purpose guns, and a mix of centerline and deck-edge lifts.
segments.summary: After locating a reference drawing, Dr. Clarke walks through the likely island, machinery, lift, gun, and displacement choices of a G3 carrier.
segments.body: The large hull supports a Lexington-scale carrier without retaining heavy cruiser guns, and the machinery position may encourage a twin-island or rearranged island solution. Dr. Clarke expects British experimentation with both centerline and deck-edge lifts and a strong dual-purpose battery rather than oversized surface armament.

evidence.note: The displayed conversion drawing supports the discussion of 35,000 tons, islands, guns, machinery, and lifts.

segments.title: Converting Hood under the treaty disadvantages every navy
segments.question: Would a treaty requiring Hood's conversion into a carrier while allowing three Nelsons be a net gain for the Royal Navy?
segments.answerShort: No; Britain would lose the centerpiece of its global battlecruiser strategy, still need a second equivalent carrier, and provoke a capital-ship ratio that also disadvantages the United States.
segments.summary: A treaty counterfactual shows why carrier parity, 16-inch battleship ratios, and Hood's global role cannot be traded independently.
segments.body: Carrier conversions were still an uncertain capability, and Britain would prefer a matched pair rather than Hood alone against two Lexingtons and two Japanese ships. Adding a third Nelson changes the 16-inch balance as well, leaving each signatory with a reason to reject the bargain.

evidence.note: The answer compares carrier parity, Hood's strategic role, and the altered 16-inch battleship ratio.

segments.title: A bilateral treaty cannot isolate Britain's global naval race
segments.question: Would Britain accept a 1908 treaty limiting Germany to two smaller capital ships per year while Britain built five larger ships?
segments.answerShort: No; the proposal addresses Germany's numbers but ignores Britain's qualitative competition with the United States, Japan, Italy, and other navies.
segments.summary: This answer distinguishes the Anglo-German quantitative race from the Royal Navy's simultaneous global qualitative competition.
segments.body: Britain could not freeze gun size and displacement solely for a bilateral bargain because its ships also had to lead or match other powers. Dr. Clarke argues that separating Germany into its own strategic sandbox misunderstands the Royal Navy's worldwide obligations and design pressures.

evidence.note: The answer contrasts the German quantitative race with qualitative competition against several other navies.

segments.title: Without Washington, Britain keeps ordering capital ships
segments.question: Would Britain have continued building beyond its existing designs without the Washington Naval Treaty?
segments.answerShort: Yes; rather than follow a single long program, British governments would keep ordering annual batches while rival American and Japanese construction remained active.
segments.summary: A procurement answer contrasts Britain's rolling annual orders with more explicit American and Japanese capital-ship programs.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke expects the Royal Navy to continue ordering roughly four ships a year as long as the international race required them. The approach also preserves shipbuilding employment, so construction would taper only after rival programs ceased rather than at a predetermined endpoint.

evidence.note: The answer describes rolling British annual orders tied to American and Japanese competition.

segments.title: Armor plate and slips constrain maximum warship output
segments.question: What would limit maximum British construction of major warships in the early twentieth century?
segments.answerShort: The decisive bottlenecks would be armor-plate supply and the number of suitable building slips; many other constraints could be worked around.
segments.summary: A concise industrial-base answer identifies the hard constraints behind theoretical British capital-ship output.
segments.body: Shipbuilding capacity is not simply a count of yards or workers. Dr. Clarke singles out heavy armor production and sufficiently large slips as the resources that cannot be expanded or substituted quickly when major-warship orders surge.

evidence.note: The answer names armor plate and building slips as the principal constraints.

segments.title: A maximum-effort empire could order about 64 destroyers yearly
segments.question: How many destroyers could Britain and the wider empire lay down annually at maximum capacity before 1939?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke estimates roughly 64 per year across Britain, Australia, Canada, India, and South Africa, while warning that completion would span about two years.
segments.summary: A rough production calculation combines shipyards across the British Empire and separates annual orders from completed hulls.
segments.body: The estimate assigns the largest share to British yards and adds smaller but meaningful contributions from dominion and Indian capacity. Because destroyers take longer than a year to build, the figure describes a sustained production flow rather than 64 finished ships appearing each year immediately.

evidence.note: The answer totals contributions from Britain, Australia, Canada, India, and South Africa and notes the two-year build cycle.

segments.title: The County class works better in Royal Navy terms
segments.question: Should the County class be understood as a cut-down heavy cruiser?
segments.answerShort: No; Dr. Clarke argues that the Royal Navy conceived it as a large light cruiser succeeding Town- and Hawkins-type ships, a frame in which the design makes more sense.
segments.summary: A classification answer explains why applying later heavy-cruiser expectations can distort the County class's intended role.
segments.body: The class was planned after the treaty framework and emerged from British cruiser thinking rather than from a preexisting heavy-cruiser ideal. Evaluated as a long-range large light cruiser, its tradeoffs are coherent; evaluated only against a heavy-cruiser stereotype, they appear deficient.

evidence.note: The answer places the County class in the British large-light-cruiser lineage.

segments.title: A fast-battleship alternative to the Admiral class
segments.question: If Britain built Hood but chose a different follow-on design, what might replace the Admiral-class battlecruiser concept?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke proposes a roughly 28-knot fast battleship with twelve 15-inch guns, or possibly eight 18-inch guns, whose stronger protection could allow all four ships to survive the post-Jutland redesign crisis.
segments.summary: This design counterfactual links Jutland lessons, triple turrets, ranging salvos, speed, and the emergence of a fast-battleship core.
segments.body: Early analysis of Jutland favored more guns for ranging salvos, making triple turrets and a twelve-gun battery attractive. A design protected as a battleship from the outset would need fewer disruptive revisions than the Admirals and could leave the Royal Navy with four fast capital ships alongside upgraded Queen Elizabeths.

evidence.note: The answer sketches a 28-knot fast battleship with triple turrets and explains why Jutland would validate rather than disrupt it.

segments.title: Royal Navy naming avoided saints and national abstractions
segments.question: What naming themes could the Royal Navy use for additional capital ships?
segments.answerShort: Classical mythology and historic admirals fit better than saint names or HMS Great Britain, which carried religious, political, and public-relations problems.
segments.summary: A naming-policy answer connects ship names to Protestant institutional culture, Irish politics, press risk, and the embarrassment of losing a nationally named vessel.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains that saint names could evoke Catholic powers and sit awkwardly with the Royal Navy's Church of England public identity, while a name such as Great Britain creates obvious headlines whenever the ship fails or is damaged. The service instead had ample mythology, admirals, battles, and traditional names to draw upon.

evidence.note: The answer explains institutional, political, and press reasons behind Royal Navy naming preferences.

segments.title: Littorio facing Lion in a delayed Second World War
segments.question: If war began in 1942, how likely is it that Italian Littorio-class battleships would face British Lion-class ships?
segments.answerShort: Quite likely, with perhaps six Lions entering service, although Italian ships could also meet modernized Queen Elizabeths, Vanguards, or King George V-class battleships.
segments.summary: A delayed-war force comparison maps the Royal Navy's planned battleship pipeline against Italy's Littorio class.
segments.body: The Royal Navy would be modernizing older ships, bringing in Lions, and using Vanguards to replace the R class while maintaining overall numbers. Dr. Clarke treats Littorio as dangerous rather than hopelessly outmatched, but emphasizes the mixed British force and possible pressure to standardize 14-, 15-, and 16-inch fleets.

evidence.note: The answer outlines likely Lion numbers, other British battleships, and the armament-standardization problem.

segments.title: Monitors sit outside the main treaty strength contest
segments.question: How would naval treaties handle monitors derived from First World War designs?
segments.answerShort: Monitors were already permitted without a dedicated strength category because they supported land and amphibious operations rather than the battle fleet.
segments.summary: A treaty answer explains why heavily armed monitors did not need a competitive tonnage allocation.
segments.body: The Royal Navy carried monitors through the Washington and London systems with notification rather than treating them as a core measure of naval power. Their slow, specialized shore-bombardment mission made them strategically different from ships intended to contest fleet control.

evidence.note: The answer says monitors were allowed because they were land-support assets rather than battle-fleet strength.

segments.title: Multiple 18-inch British ships rewrite the treaty ceiling
segments.question: How would an 18-inch British fast-battleship class affect the Washington Naval Treaty?
segments.answerShort: Unlike one-off Hood, four 18-inch fast battleships would force American and Japanese equivalents and could push the treaty limit toward 18-inch guns and about 45,000 tons.
segments.summary: A capital-ship counterfactual shows how a class of fast 18-inch ships could shift both construction programs and treaty ratios.
segments.body: A battleship-protected design would be validated rather than delayed by Jutland, allowing several ships to complete by 1918-1919 and a successor class to begin. With multiple examples in service, negotiators could not dismiss the design as a unique exception and would have to recognize a higher international standard.

evidence.note: The answer links four completed fast battleships to American and Japanese responses and a higher treaty limit.

segments.title: Triple turrets on Tiger accelerate capital-ship firepower
segments.question: What changes if HMS Tiger introduces triple main-gun turrets for later British capital ships?
segments.answerShort: Later British ships could carry twelve 15-inch guns and perhaps reach 28 knots, while Germany would face the costly burden of developing both larger guns and triple turrets.
segments.summary: A turret counterfactual follows Tiger's influence into Queen Elizabeth-type ships and the wider Anglo-German arms race.
segments.body: More turrets and guns enlarge the hull but also create room for machinery, potentially producing a faster twelve-gun Queen Elizabeth class. The German response is especially difficult because its army-dominated budget would have to fund simultaneous gun and mounting development to keep pace.

evidence.note: The answer explores British twelve-gun ships, higher speed, and German development costs.

segments.title: Naval treaty policy was a cross-party choice
segments.question: Which British politicians should be blamed for interwar naval treaties and declining shipbuilding capacity?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke assigns collective responsibility: Labour strongly supported the 1930 London Treaty, while Conservative governments had accepted the Washington framework.
segments.summary: A political answer rejects a single-party explanation for Britain's interwar naval limits and industrial contraction.
segments.body: The treaty system survived changes of government because several parties accepted its financial and diplomatic logic. Dr. Clarke therefore treats the loss of capacity as a collective policy outcome rather than the work of one minister or party.

evidence.note: The answer attributes the treaty sequence to both Labour and Conservative governments.

segments.title: The Strait of Messina needed denial more than battleship guns
segments.question: Did Italy station battleship-caliber guns at the Strait of Messina, and what difference would such batteries make?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke does not identify major-caliber batteries there and argues that the narrow, easily blocked strait made other defenses adequate; batteries near Marsala or farther west could be more strategically troublesome.
segments.summary: A coastal-defense answer distinguishes the tactically narrow Messina strait from more useful artillery positions covering wider Mediterranean approaches.
segments.body: Heavy and light anti-aircraft guns, castles, and the geography already discouraged hostile passage through Messina. Dr. Clarke cautions that he has not made a specialist study of the site, but suggests that long-range batteries covering broader sea lanes would matter more than oversized guns in the narrows.

evidence.note: The answer discusses Messina's geography, existing defenses, and more strategically useful western battery locations.

segments.title: Guaranteeing a one-hit battleship disablement takes extreme weapons
segments.question: What torpedo warhead or shell could disable a 1920s-1940s battleship with one waterline hit?
segments.answerShort: A guaranteed single-hit disablement would require something like a 24-inch torpedo against weak underwater protection or an 18-inch heavy shell, and even then more than one hit is preferable.
segments.summary: A damage-resistance answer sets the scale of weapon needed to guarantee rather than merely risk disabling a battleship.
segments.body: Battleships vary too much in underwater protection and internal subdivision for a modest weapon to promise a one-shot result. Dr. Clarke therefore gives deliberately extreme examples and stresses that operational planners would still want multiple damaging hits.

evidence.note: The answer gives 24-inch torpedo and 18-inch shell examples while cautioning against a guaranteed one-hit assumption.

segments.title: Italy could challenge only a carefully limited British squadron
segments.question: Did the Italian Navy plan to defeat the Royal Navy in a decisive central Mediterranean battle?
segments.answerShort: Not in a robust sense; Italian calculations depended on meeting a limited squadron of older British ships without Nelson, Rodney, or modernized Queen Elizabeths.
segments.summary: A fleet-planning answer shows how an Italian battle concept depended on the Royal Navy sending exactly the wrong ships.
segments.body: Italian planners could imagine holding off a selected British squadron, but not the full range of forces Britain could deploy. Dr. Clarke calls a plan that works only if the enemy chooses specific weaker ships a dream rather than a dependable strategy.

evidence.note: The answer lists the British ships that would upset the Italian scenario and rejects it as a reliable decisive-battle plan.

segments.title: Vanguard still fits a fleet that receives the Lion class
segments.question: Would HMS Vanguard still be built if the Lion-class battleships entered service?
segments.answerShort: Yes; Vanguard was part of a plan to expand toward 20-25 or ideally 27 capital ships while replacing the least modernizable R-class battleships.
segments.summary: A force-planning answer places Vanguard and Lion in different parts of the Royal Navy's intended battleship expansion.
segments.body: The King George V class grows the fleet, Lions add modern 16-inch ships, and Vanguards replace R-class hulls that cannot be modernized economically. The target supports battle squadrons in the Atlantic, Mediterranean, and Indian Ocean, ideally with eight ships and a flagship in each theater.

evidence.note: The answer counts existing and planned ships and explains Vanguard's R-class replacement role.

segments.title: A G3 carrier could operate roughly 96 aircraft
segments.question: How many aircraft would a G3 carrier conversion carry, and would it use a double hangar?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke expects a single hangar and about 96 aircraft, with a notional mix of fighters, dive bombers, and a larger torpedo-reconnaissance component.
segments.summary: An air-group estimate links a G3 conversion's hangar arrangement to Fleet Air Arm squadron organization.
segments.body: British accounting would exclude deck parking and aircraft stored overhead, while allocating more space to maintenance than some foreign designs. A group of about 96 could encourage larger 24-aircraft squadrons instead of the smaller formations the Fleet Air Arm historically used.

evidence.note: The answer estimates a single hangar, 96 aircraft, and discusses squadron composition.

segments.title: Two Mackensens yearly trigger a much larger British program
segments.question: How would Britain respond if Germany built two modified Mackensen-class battlecruisers with triple turrets every year from 1908?
segments.answerShort: Britain would adopt triple turrets, expand battlecruiser output, and perhaps build four battlecruisers plus six battleships annually to preserve superiority.
segments.summary: A naval-arms-race answer connects a sustained German battlecruiser program to British turret development and ten-capital-ship annual output.
segments.body: Britain must answer German battlecruisers while also maintaining a battleship lead over other powers, so the response cannot be a simple one-for-one match. Dr. Clarke judges that British industry could sustain the resulting four-plus-six program if the political requirement existed.

evidence.note: The answer projects British triple turrets and a possible six-battleship, four-battlecruiser annual program.

segments.title: Visby could inform LCS but not meet the requirement unchanged
segments.question: How close could a large Visby-class design come to the US Navy's Littoral Combat Ship requirement?
segments.answerShort: Closer than LCS advocates might admit but farther than Visby advocates claim, because it lacks payload and does not fit American crewing standards without major redesign.
segments.summary: A procurement comparison separates a promising foreign design concept from the changes required for US Navy service.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke says an enlarged Visby deserved serious consideration, but its combat punch and accommodation assumptions do not directly satisfy American requirements. The choice would be to alter US crewing expectations or redesign the ship, and he expects the United States to choose the latter.

evidence.note: The answer identifies payload and American crewing standards as the main gaps.

segments.title: An early St. Lawrence Seaway expands protected war production
segments.question: How would completing the St. Lawrence Seaway before 1939 affect the Second World War and Great Lakes industry?
segments.answerShort: It would enlarge protected industrial and training capacity around the Great Lakes and simplify movement of aircraft and other matériel between the United States and Canada.
segments.summary: A logistics counterfactual explains how ocean access to the Great Lakes could shelter production and ease Allied cross-border supply.
segments.body: The Seaway would make more shipbuilding and training possible far from enemy action while strengthening the regional industrial base. It could also replace awkward border-transfer arrangements with barge and water transport for aircraft and other supplies.

evidence.note: The answer discusses protected Great Lakes construction, training, and easier US-Canadian equipment transfer.

segments.title: Fisher's Baltic plan was feasible with the right battle fleet
segments.question: Was Jackie Fisher's plan to force a way into the Baltic actually feasible?
segments.answerShort: Yes, though not in its usual form; Dr. Clarke and Andrew Lambert concluded that success needed numerous 28-knot Queen Elizabeth-class battleships able to break through before Germany concentrated.
segments.summary: A strategic answer distinguishes the feasibility of a Baltic operation from the weaknesses of Fisher's proposed execution.
segments.body: Speed and concentrated battle-fleet strength are the keys: the British force must pass the narrows before German battlecruisers can delay it and before the High Seas Fleet can defeat divided elements. Once a strong force is through, Germany faces the harder choice of contesting the straits against an established fleet.

evidence.note: The answer says the plan is feasible with numerous fast Queen Elizabeths and rapid concentration through the straits.

segments.title: Earlier snorkels change fleet building more than ASW doctrine
segments.question: How would widespread snorkels in the 1930s change submarine development and anti-submarine warfare?
segments.answerShort: More navies would build snorkel submarines and more escorts, but existing confidence in ASDIC would delay doctrinal change; German output would still be constrained by dysfunctional procurement.
segments.summary: A long answer connects early snorkels to escort construction, ASDIC assumptions, German production failures, and the tactical value of numerous maneuverable guns.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke expects larger submarine and sloop programs rather than an immediate revolution in detection doctrine, because navies believed their sonar problems were manageable. He uses German procurement and a Tribal-versus-German-destroyer comparison to show why a small number of impressive weapons cannot compensate for inadequate numbers or weapons unsuited to close maneuvering combat.

evidence.note: The answer covers snorkel adoption, ASDIC confidence, escort numbers, German procurement, and destroyer gun tactics.

segments.title: A museum carrier larger than Midway depends on US choices
segments.question: Will a ship as large as or larger than USS Midway ever be preserved as a museum?
segments.answerShort: Only the United States is likely to have a candidate, but the loss of nuclear Enterprise makes preservation doubtful unless a future ship gains exceptional wartime significance.
segments.summary: A preservation answer compares the prospects for supercarrier museums with the decisions made over USS Enterprise and Falklands-era British ships.
segments.body: Size, nuclear systems, cost, and the absence of an extraordinary public claim all work against preserving a modern supercarrier. Dr. Clarke treats Enterprise's disposal as evidence that routine historical importance may not be enough for the next generation of very large museum ships.

evidence.note: The answer points to USS Enterprise and the scarcity of preserved Falklands-era ships.

segments.title: Britain could have made Heligoland a formidable fortress
segments.question: Could Britain have retained and defended Heligoland into the First World War?
segments.answerShort: Yes; concrete, guns, stores, and the island's physical resilience could make it extremely difficult to seize, although Britain historically transferred it to improve relations with Germany.
segments.summary: A strategic-geography answer imagines Heligoland as a heavily fortified British outpost rather than a diplomatic concession.
segments.body: The same hard geology that later complicated demolition would support deep fortifications and powerful coastal batteries. Dr. Clarke contrasts that military potential with the political purpose of the transfer, which Britain intended as reassurance but Germany interpreted as recognition of its status.

evidence.note: The answer discusses fortification potential and the failed diplomatic signal behind the island's transfer.

segments.title: Taking the Sea Fury back to 1939
segments.question: Which late-war carrier aircraft would make the greatest difference if available at the start of the Second World War?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke chooses the Hawker Sea Fury for its carrier compatibility, 400-knot speed, four 20 mm cannon, rockets, bomb load, and long range.
segments.summary: An aircraft counterfactual compares the Avenger and Firebrand before selecting the Sea Fury as the most disruptive early-war Fleet Air Arm fighter.
segments.body: The Swordfish already met early-war needs, while the Sea Fury combines exceptional fighter performance with a naval airframe that can fit contemporary carriers. Its speed, armament, strike load, and range would transform air defense and offensive operations from Norway to the Mediterranean.

evidence.note: The answer compares candidate aircraft and details the Sea Fury's speed, guns, rockets, bombs, and range.

segments.title: Naval control helps Coastal Command only with continuity
segments.question: Would RAF Coastal Command have worked better under Royal Navy control?
segments.answerShort: Probably only if naval control continued from the First World War; a late transfer would force the Navy to rebuild personnel and procedures nearly from scratch.
segments.summary: An institutional answer distinguishes continuous naval ownership from a disruptive interwar takeover of maritime aviation.
segments.body: A continuous Royal Navy organization could preserve officers, doctrine, and operational understanding from the First World War. A transfer in 1927 or 1939 would not create instant competence, though it might reduce the mismatch between what the fleet expected and what air leadership communicated.

evidence.note: The answer contrasts institutional continuity with the costs of a late transfer.

segments.title: A lost Royal Navy museum fleet
segments.question: Which ten Royal Navy ships scrapped after the Second World War should have been preserved, and where?
segments.answerShort: Dr. Clarke prioritizes the four surviving British Tribals, Warspite, Illustrious, Renown, Furious, and representative cruisers, distributed among accessible ports around Britain and Freetown.
segments.summary: A museum-fleet answer pairs significant destroyers, battleships, carriers, and cruisers with regional ports rather than concentrating every ship at Portsmouth.
segments.body: The list preserves both famous individual careers and otherwise-lost ship types, including a Tribal in Africa and Warspite in Cornwall. Dr. Clarke favors a national network across Portsmouth, Liverpool, Newcastle, Cornwall, Scotland, and Freetown so maritime heritage remains geographically connected to naval communities.

evidence.note: The answer names candidate Tribals, capital ships, carriers, cruisers, and several preservation locations.

segments.title: Balancing armor on a 9.2-inch treaty cruiser
segments.question: Is a 4.5-5.5-inch belt, 2.5-inch deck, and 2-inch turret armor reasonable for a 15,500-ton cruiser with 9.2-inch guns?
segments.answerShort: Broadly yes, with a 4.5-inch belt and roughly 2.5-3 inches of deck protection, while spare plate could reinforce turret faces, sides, and roofs.
segments.summary: A cruiser-armor answer allocates limited protection among the belt, deck, magazines, and turrets.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke accepts the proposed scheme as a workable baseline for the displacement. He suggests that available plate would likely be concentrated around the forward faces and exposed sides of the turrets after the belt and deck requirements were met.

evidence.note: The answer endorses the belt and deck range and identifies likely turret reinforcement.

segments.title: How the Royal Marines handled non-Commando personnel
segments.question: What happened to Royal Marines who could not complete the Commando course after the corps adopted the Commando role?
segments.answerShort: Most qualified; those who did not could move to staff duties, retire, or transfer, while postwar demobilization and force reductions absorbed much of the issue.
segments.summary: An organizational answer explains the Royal Marines' pre-Commando roles and the personnel pathways available after conversion.
segments.body: Before the full Commando identity, Royal Marines served in ships' detachments, gunnery roles, port defense, and mobile naval-base organizations rather than a simple infantry-battalion structure. The high qualification rate and postwar reductions meant the remaining personnel cases could be handled individually.

evidence.note: The answer outlines earlier Marine roles and the staff, retirement, transfer, and demobilization options.

segments.title: Courageous cannot be enlarged into a 35,000-ton carrier
segments.question: Could the Courageous-class conversions use the treaty's 35,000-ton allowance to become much larger carriers?
segments.answerShort: No; the original hulls were about 19,000 tons standard and gained roughly 5,000 tons in conversion, so adding another 10,000-15,000 tons would overstress the structure and amount to building a new ship.
segments.summary: A conversion calculation explains why treaty allowance does not create usable structural capacity in an existing hull.
segments.body: Removing guns and rearranging armor provides only limited weight for a flight deck, island, hangar, and aviation systems. Admiral- or G3-class hulls could plausibly approach the 35,000-ton carrier category, but Courageous and Glorious lack the framing and volume for that scale of growth.

evidence.note: The answer compares original, converted, and proposed displacement and identifies structural overstress.

segments.title: Four triple turrets beat a mixed German battery
segments.question: If Mackensen used a triple-quadruple-triple layout, would Britain answer with all-quadruple turrets?
segments.answerShort: Probably not; four triple turrets still provide twelve guns and preserve more firepower after a turret casualty than three concentrated mountings.
segments.summary: A turret-layout answer compares gun count with the Royal Navy's preference for distributing firepower across four mountings.
segments.body: The German arrangement adds only one gun over a ten-gun pattern, while a British four-triple design retains a numerical advantage. More importantly, losing one of four turrets costs 25 percent of the battery rather than roughly a third, matching British survivability preferences.

evidence.note: The answer compares twelve versus ten guns and the percentage of firepower lost with one turret.

segments.title: Funding, housing, and local reserves are central Royal Navy needs
segments.question: What should the Royal Navy do to address its current problems?
segments.answerShort: Increase funding and fleet size, improve pay and family housing, and make reserve service easier through accessible local units and fewer administrative barriers.
segments.summary: A personnel-policy answer links fleet growth to the practical offer made to regular sailors, families, and reservists.
segments.body: Recruitment and retention depend on housing, compensation, and a credible service life rather than slogans alone. Dr. Clarke uses Cornwall's distance from HMS Vivid despite the presence of RNAS Culdrose to show how missing local reserve units can exclude otherwise willing personnel.

evidence.note: The answer covers funding, housing, pay, reserve administration, and the Cornwall basing example.

segments.title: Recovering Satō Tetsutarō's naval thought from a Japanese text
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke introduces a hard-won Japanese-language book and a plan to use photography and OCR to study Satō Tetsutarō, a major Japanese naval theorist.
segments.body: The book story shows the practical barriers to naval-history research when an important text is scarce and untranslated. Dr. Clarke and Drachinifel plan to photograph the volume, apply Japanese OCR, and build an English working version that could support a future video on Satō as a Japanese counterpart to Mahan.

evidence.note: The book tale describes acquiring the Japanese text, photographing it, using OCR, and researching Satō Tetsutarō.

segments.title: Courageous was a Baltic large light cruiser, not a normal battlecruiser
segments.question: Can Courageous and Glorious be treated as ordinary battlecruiser designs outside Fisher's Baltic plan?
segments.answerShort: No; they were specialized large light cruisers built for the Baltic concept, much smaller than Tiger and too lightly armed for long-range battlecruiser gunnery.
segments.summary: A sustained design answer separates Fisher's Courageous class from the Royal Navy's missing 1915-1916 conventional battlecruiser work.
segments.body: The class is an aberration tied to a specific operational scheme, not the next step after Tiger or Renown. Calling the ships battlecruisers obscures their low displacement, inadequate ranging battery, shortened 18-inch weapons on Furious, and closer relationship to an oversized heavy-cruiser concept.

evidence.note: The answer contrasts the Baltic large-light-cruiser design with conventional battlecruiser requirements and unknown wartime projects.

segments.title: Iowa answered Kirov with visible naval presence
segments.question: Would the Soviets have built still larger guided-missile cruisers if the United States had matched Kirov directly?
segments.answerShort: Possibly, but recommissioned Iowa-class battleships ended the visual competition by providing a more imposing symbol of power in port than concealed missile batteries.
segments.summary: A Cold War comparison distinguishes destructive missile capacity from the visible political effect of nine 16-inch guns.
segments.body: Kirov could carry enough missiles to devastate a target, but vertical launch cells do not display their power to observers. Iowa's large turrets made an immediate presence argument, giving the United States a prestige response without designing an even larger missile cruiser.

evidence.note: The answer contrasts concealed Kirov missiles with the visible presence of Iowa's 16-inch battery.

segments.title: Modern destroyers and frigates inherited cruiser missions
segments.question: Why has cruiser development declined while destroyers are overworked?
segments.answerShort: The cruiser name carries imperial associations, while large air-defense destroyers and anti-submarine frigates now perform the presence and cruising missions once assigned to cruisers.
segments.summary: A classification answer explains how modern surface combatants grew into cruiser roles without keeping the cruiser label.
segments.body: Today's air-defense destroyer is functionally an air-defense cruiser, and the anti-submarine frigate often serves as another specialized cruiser. Names changed for political and institutional reasons even as hull size, endurance, and presence duties expanded.

evidence.note: The answer maps air-defense destroyers and ASW frigates onto traditional cruiser missions.

segments.title: A conventional 1915 battlecruiser develops Tiger and Renown
segments.question: Would a conventional 1915-1916 British battlecruiser be an enlarged Renown or an early Admiral type?
segments.answerShort: It would likely combine Tiger's four-turret lineage with Renown-era machinery and armament in a roughly 31,000-32,000-ton design.
segments.summary: A missing-design reconstruction uses the progression from Lion and Queen Mary through Tiger and Renown to estimate a wartime successor.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke expects four turrets in an AB-plus-aft arrangement and a hull that looks more like a scaled Tiger than a direct battleship adaptation. Extrapolating the steady displacement growth places the conventional successor around 31,000 tons normal load.

evidence.note: The answer compares Tiger, Renown, Queen Mary, and Lion dimensions to estimate the missing design.

segments.title: Modern warships need active defenses and physical protection
segments.question: What is the modern equivalent of naval armor: close-in weapons, electronic systems, or passive materials?
segments.answerShort: Both active and passive protection are necessary: layered defenses keep threats away, while armor and subdivision reduce damage when something gets through.
segments.summary: A concise survivability answer rejects choosing between interception systems and structural protection.
segments.body: Active defenses should engage threats at the greatest possible distance and continue through close-in layers. Because no interception system is perfect, key spaces still need physical protection, redundancy, and subdivision to contain damage.

evidence.note: The answer explicitly calls for both active defenses and armor around key points with subdivision.

segments.title: Soviet battleships in 1950 revive Western capital-ship building
segments.question: How would the West respond if the Soviet Union commissioned several battleship squadrons in 1950?
segments.answerShort: The United States would likely complete Illinois and Kentucky, Britain would revive Super Lion work, and Western navies would retain or finish more battleships alongside carriers.
segments.summary: A Cold War counterfactual follows surviving shipyard capacity and incomplete hulls into a renewed capital-ship race.
segments.body: The response would begin before the Soviet ships commissioned because their construction would be visible. With Iowa hulls still extant, Vanguard recently completed, and design staffs still available, Western powers could react by finishing ships and forming combined battleship-carrier groups rather than starting from nothing.

evidence.note: The answer identifies Illinois, Kentucky, Super Lion, retained French ships, and combined battle groups.

segments.title: Ships are lengthened by inserting a new midbody
segments.question: How and where are ships lengthened by adding a middle section?
segments.answerShort: The cut-and-insert operation is normally done in a dry dock, sometimes a floating dock, and remains common on large commercial vessels as well as historic warship rebuilds.
segments.summary: A shipyard-method answer explains the continuing practice of cutting a hull and inserting a prefabricated midbody.
segments.body: The work requires dock support so the separated hull sections can be aligned and joined safely. Dr. Clarke notes that the method is not an exotic historical trick: modern cruise ships still receive major capacity increases through comparable midbody insertions.

evidence.note: The answer identifies dry docks and modern cruise-ship cut-and-insert work.

segments.title: Later missiles preserve balanced gun batteries
segments.question: If missiles arrived in the late 1980s or early 1990s, how many more gunships would modern navies operate?
segments.answerShort: Navies would field more gun-balanced ships rather than true gunships, retaining twin medium-caliber mounts on destroyers and frigates for longer.
segments.summary: A technology-timeline answer imagines modern surface combatants keeping multiple gun mounts because missile systems mature two decades later.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke expects the main effect among medium navies and ships such as the Type 42 or a modern Daring, not a return to battleship-style gun platforms. Falklands-era experience would reinforce the utility of twin 4.5-inch mounts while missiles remained immature.

evidence.note: The answer predicts gun-balanced surface combatants and continued twin medium-caliber mounts.

segments.title: Missile conversion depends on a cruiser's remaining life
segments.question: Would Britain convert heavy cruisers into guided-missile ships or prefer new construction?
segments.answerShort: Old County-class hulls would favor new construction, but a larger 9.2-inch cruiser built near the war's end might justify conversion because it retained decades of useful life.
segments.summary: A conversion-policy answer makes hull age and remaining service life more important than the heavy-cruiser label.
segments.body: Missile conversion requires volume, stability, and enough future service to repay a costly rebuild. Dr. Clarke therefore rejects aging treaty cruisers but accepts that a younger, larger wartime design could provide a practical platform.

evidence.note: The answer contrasts old County-class hulls with a younger large 9.2-inch cruiser.

segments.title: A slower Deutschland gains only modest armor
segments.question: What would the Deutschland class look like if designed as a 22-knot coastal battleship?
segments.answerShort: Armament would stay similar and the belt might grow from about 3.1 inches to 4-5 inches, but six knots of sacrificed speed does not free enough weight for a dramatic transformation.
segments.summary: A design answer tests whether slowing the Deutschland class can turn it into a genuinely well-protected coastal battleship.
segments.body: The diesel machinery was not heavy enough for a modest speed reduction to buy a new turret or battleship-level protection. Dr. Clarke frames the historical design as a serviceable product of damaged German infrastructure, but not the exceptional ship later publicity implied.

evidence.note: The answer compares speed, machinery, belt thickness, and the limited weight available for redesign.

segments.title: Recruitment shortfalls reflect policy and career conditions
segments.question: Do small modern armed forces amid large populations show a lack of patriotism?
segments.answerShort: No; force size reflects government funding, recruiting systems, pay, career prospects, and medical rules more than public willingness to serve.
segments.summary: A personnel answer compares European recruitment problems with the US military's clearer career and social-mobility offer.
segments.body: People weigh military service against safer civilian work, especially when pay, promotion, housing, and long-term prospects are uncertain. Dr. Clarke argues that governments created many shortages by shrinking forces, outsourcing slow recruitment, and excluding candidates on remote future-health risks.

evidence.note: The answer discusses budgets, careers, social mobility, comparative pay, and restrictive recruitment rules.

segments.title: Early missile-cruiser conversions taught space and balance
segments.question: Were Boston, Galveston, Albany, and Providence merely stopgaps, and what did the US Navy learn from them?
segments.answerShort: They were evolutionary test beds that taught the Navy to reserve space, manage weight and balance, and plan crew movement before purpose-built missile cruisers.
segments.summary: A technology-development answer presents converted cruisers as learning platforms rather than disposable interim ships.
segments.body: The conversions exposed how large missile systems, radars, and magazines reshape an existing hull. Lessons about topweight, internal circulation, and usable volume fed into later ships designed around guided weapons from the keel up.

evidence.note: The answer calls the conversions an evolutionary path and lists space, balance, and crew-circulation lessons.

segments.title: Large dockyards and G3 carriers accelerate British naval aviation
segments.question: How would larger British dockyards and treaty-converted G3 carriers affect later carrier design?
segments.answerShort: They would make large carriers easier and faster to build, enlarge later designs, encourage twin-engine carrier aircraft before the war, and complicate treaty limits by creating several undeniable large capital hulls.
segments.summary: The final Q&A ties dock dimensions, G3 or Admiral conversions, aircraft development, and treaty politics into one carrier-development path.
segments.body: A fleet operating several G3-scale carriers would demand larger aircraft, maintenance spaces, and follow-on hulls rather than treating a single ship as an exception. Improved docks reduce construction friction, while multiple completed Admiral-class or G3 hulls force treaty negotiators to account for them directly.

evidence.note: The answer links 1,000-foot dock capacity, G3 carriers, twin-engine aircraft, larger follow-ons, and treaty consequences.
